---
title: Interactive Python
format: 
  live-html:
    theme:
      light: [flatly, ../../styles.scss]
      dark: [darkly, ../../styles.scss]
    css: ../../styles.css
    toc: true
    mainfont: "inter, sans-serif"
    monofont: "JetBrains Mono, monospace"
---

Some websites let learners execute Python directly in the browser. This is
useful for short exploratory tasks where immediate feedback is more important
than distributing a full notebook.

## Browser-executed cells

The `imol` course site uses configured Quarto live/Pyodide support for cells
written like this:

<pre><code>```&#123;pyodide&#125;
1 + 2 + 3
```</code></pre>

When rendered by that configured project, the cell becomes an interactive
Python area in the HTML page. Like shown below

```{pyodide}
1 + 2 + 3
```

These blocks rely on [pyodide](https://pyodide.org/en/stable/)
which is a Python distribution for the browser based on WebAssembly. This allows 
Python to run in the browser without the need for a server as it uses the local machine (be it a laptop, desktop or a cellphone).

::: {.callout-warning title="Project-dependent feature"}
`pyodide` cells require the appropriate Quarto extension and output format.
Adding the syntax to an unrelated course repository does not by itself enable
browser execution.
:::

## Enabling interactive Python

To best way to enable interactive Python is to use the [Quarto Live](https://r-wasm.github.io/quarto-live/)-extension. For a new project where it is not already enabled this requires running the command

```default
pixi run quarto add r-wasm/quarto-live
```
From the Quarto root directory which is where the `_quarto.yaml` is located (and typically not the repository root.). This will add an `_extensions`-directory (if one doesn't already exist)

```
_extensions
└── r-wasm
```

After this it can be enabled for a specific `.qmd`-source document by changing the format in the header, like 

```yaml
---
title: Interactive Python
format: 
  live-html:
    theme:
      light: [flatly, ../../styles.scss]
      dark: [darkly, ../../styles.scss]
    css: ../../styles.css
    toc: true
    mainfont: "inter, sans-serif"
    monofont: "JetBrains Mono, monospace"
---
```

The important part is to change the format to `live-html` the rest is just to apply consistent styling 
compared to the other pages that have their HTML styling configured in `_quarto.yml`.

## Exercises 

The Quarto Live extension enables adding exercises with accompanying solution code, such that 
exercises are automatically checked. See [Quarto Live: Creating Exercises](https://r-wasm.github.io/quarto-live/exercises/exercises.html) for more information. Below is an example of such an exercise. 


### Example

Fill in the blank so the result is 10

```{pyodide}
#| exercise: ex_1
#| check: true

if total == 10:
    feedback = {"correct": True, "message": "That's correct!"}
else:
    feedback = {"correct": False, "message": "That's not quite correct"}

feedback
```


```{pyodide}
#| exercise: ex_1
total = 1 + 2 + 3 + ______
print(total)
```

## When to use interactive Python

Interactive cells suit small calculations, quick data exploration, and
low-setup introductions to programming. Prefer rendered notebooks or a normal
execution workflow when learners need:

- Larger code workflows or many interdependent cells.
- Installed packages not supported by the in-browser environment.
- Persistent files, saved work, or assessment submission.

## Authoring checks

Preview the actual website output after editing an interactive cell. Confirm
that the cell loads, accepts input, executes expected examples, and still
works in the page layout used for learners.
